title_en: Paradise and Hellfire — Their Reality and Permanence
title_id: Surga dan Neraka — Realitas dan Kekekalannya
content_id:
Realitas, keberadaan saat ini, dan kekekalan abadi surga (al-Jannah) dan neraka (al-Nar) adalah doktrin-doktrin fundamental dari al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah. Al-Tahawi menyatakan secara tegas: 'Surga dan neraka adalah ciptaan yang sudah ada dan tidak akan pernah sirna. Allah menciptakan surga dan neraka sebelum menciptakan yang lain, dan Dia menciptakan penghuni untuk masing-masing dari keduanya.' Penegasan ini menjawab beberapa posisi teologis yang dipegang oleh berbagai kelompok sepanjang sejarah Islam.

Doktrin Sunni bahwa surga dan neraka sudah diciptakan — ada sekarang, bukan sekadar keadaan potensial yang akan diwujudkan pada Hari Pembalasan — berasal dari ayat-ayat Al-Qur'an yang jelas dan hadis-hadis sahih. Al-Qur'an berbicara tentang surga sebagai realitas yang telah ada yang 'telah dipersiapkan' (u'iddat) bagi orang-orang yang bertakwa (QS. Ali 'Imran: 133), menggunakan kata kerja lampau untuk menunjukkan keberadaan saat ini. Nabi shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam, selama Isra' dan Mi'raj, secara langsung menyaksikan surga dan neraka. Hadis tentang salat gerhana (shalat al-kusuf) menggambarkan bagaimana Nabi shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam melangkah maju kemudian mundur saat salat, dan setelahnya menjelaskan bahwa beliau telah melihat surga dan neraka, dan hampir meraih setandan anggur dari surga.

Mengenai kekekalan, al-Tahawi menegaskan konsensus Sunni bahwa surga dan neraka adalah abadi. Ini adalah salah satu perkara aqidah Islam yang paling kukuh — Al-Qur'an berulang kali menggunakan kata 'khulud' (kediaman abadi) untuk menggambarkan nasib akhir penghuni surga maupun penghuni neraka. Penghuni surga akan menetap di dalamnya selamanya, dengan kebahagiaan dan kedekatan kepada Allah yang terus meningkat. Mayoritas ulama Ahl al-Sunnah berpendapat bahwa orang-orang beriman yang masuk neraka (mereka yang berbuat dosa dan tidak diampuni di dunia ini) pada akhirnya akan dikeluarkan oleh rahmat Allah dan syafaat Nabi shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam, tetapi orang-orang kafir dan munafik yang meninggal dalam keadaan kufur akan menetap di neraka selamanya.

Satu pendirian yang ditolak al-Tahawi — meskipun ia membahasnya secara implisit — adalah pendirian mereka yang berpendapat bahwa neraka pada akhirnya akan sirna. Ini adalah pendirian yang dinisbatkan kepada beberapa ulama, termasuk Ibnu Taimiyyah dan Ibnu al-Qayyim dalam sebagian tulisan mereka, meskipun penisbatan ini diperdebatkan. Pendirian Sunni arus utama, sebagaimana diartikulasikan al-Tahawi, adalah bahwa kedua tempat kediaman itu abadi dan tidak akan musnah.

Deskripsi surga dalam Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah ditegaskan secara harfiah dan tanpa tafsir metaforis yang dipaksakan: sungai-sungai air, susu, anggur (yang tidak memabukkan), dan madu; taman-taman subur dan istana-istana megah; pertemuan dengan orang-orang yang dicintai; mendengar sapaan langsung Allah; dan di atas segalanya, Ru'yatullah. Demikian pula, deskripsi neraka — panas teriknya, makanannya berupa zaqqum, minumannya berupa air mendidih, berbagai siksaannya — ditegaskan sebagai nyata. Ahl al-Sunnah tidak 'menspiritualisasikan' deskripsi-deskripsi ini tetapi menerimanya sebagai gambaran autentik tentang realitas yang menanti, sebanding dengan dan konsisten dengan keadilan ilahi dan rahmat ilahi.
content_en:
The reality, present existence, and eternal permanence of Paradise (al-Jannah) and Hellfire (al-Nar) are foundational doctrines of al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah. Al-Tahawi states explicitly: 'Paradise and Hellfire are created and already exist. They will never cease. Allah created Paradise and Hellfire before the rest of creation, and He created inhabitants for each of them.' This affirmation addresses several theological positions that have been held by various groups throughout Islamic history.

The Sunni doctrine that Paradise and Hellfire are already created — existing right now, not merely potential states to be brought into existence on the Day of Judgment — is derived from clear Quranic verses and authentic hadiths. The Quran speaks of Paradise as an existing reality that 'has been prepared' (u'iddat) for the righteous (Quran 3:133), using the past tense to indicate present existence. The Prophet ﷺ, during the Night Journey and Ascension, witnessed both Paradise and Hellfire directly. The hadith of the eclipse prayer (salah al-kusuf) describes how the Prophet ﷺ stepped forward and then stepped back during the prayer, and afterward explained that he had seen Paradise and Hellfire, and nearly reached out to take a cluster of grapes from Paradise.

On the question of permanence, al-Tahawi affirms the Sunni consensus that both Paradise and Hellfire are eternal. This is one of the most firmly established matters of Islamic aqeedah — the Quran repeatedly uses the word 'khulud' (eternal abiding) to describe the ultimate fate of both the people of Paradise and the people of Hellfire. The people of Paradise will abide in it eternally, with ever-increasing joy and proximity to Allah. Most scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah hold that the believers in Hellfire (those who sinned and were not forgiven in this life) will eventually be removed by Allah's mercy and the intercession of the Prophet ﷺ, but the disbelievers and hypocrites who died in kufr will remain in Hellfire eternally.

A position rejected by al-Tahawi — though he addresses it implicitly — is that of those who held that Hellfire will eventually cease. This was a position attributed to some scholars, including Ibn Taymiyyah and Ibn al-Qayyim in certain of their writings, though this attribution is disputed. The mainstream Sunni position, as al-Tahawi articulates, is that both abodes are eternal and neither will perish.

The descriptions of Paradise in the Quran and Sunnah are affirmed literally and without forced metaphorical interpretation: rivers of water, milk, wine (which does not intoxicate), and honey; lush gardens and towering palaces; the meeting of loved ones; the hearing of Allah's direct address; and above all else, the Beatific Vision of Allah Himself. Similarly, the descriptions of Hellfire — its intense heat, its food of zaqqum, its drink of boiling water, its various punishments — are affirmed as real. Ahl al-Sunnah do not 'spiritualize' these descriptions away but accept them as genuine accounts of a reality that awaits, proportionate to and consistent with divine justice and divine mercy.

The reality, present existence, and eternal permanence of Paradise (al-Jannah) and Hellfire (al-Nar) are foundational doctrines of al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah. Al-Tahawi states explicitly: 'Paradise and Hellfire are created and already exist. They will never cease. الله created Paradise and Hellfire before the rest of creation, and He created inhabitants for each of them.' This affirmation addresses several theological positions that have been held by various groups throughout Islamic history.

The Sunni doctrine that Paradise and Hellfire are already created — existing right now, not merely potential states to be brought into existence on the Day of Judgment — is derived from clear Quranic verses and authentic hadiths. The Quran speaks of Paradise as an existing reality that 'has been prepared' (u'iddat) for the righteous (Quran 3:133), using the past tense to indicate present existence. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, during the Night Journey and Ascension, witnessed both Paradise and Hellfire directly. The hadith of the eclipse prayer (salah al-kusuf) describes how النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم صلى الله عليه وسلم stepped forward and then stepped back during the prayer, and afterward explained that he had seen Paradise and Hellfire, and nearly reached out to take a cluster of grapes from Paradise.

On the question of permanence, al-Tahawi affirms the Sunni consensus that both Paradise and Hellfire are eternal. This is one of the most firmly established matters of Islamic aqeedah — the Quran repeatedly uses the word 'khulud' (eternal abiding) to describe the ultimate fate of both the people of Paradise and the people of Hellfire. The people of Paradise will abide in it eternally, with ever-increasing joy and proximity to الله. Most scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah hold that the believers in Hellfire (those who sinned and were not forgiven in this life) will eventually be removed by الله's mercy and the intercession of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم صلى الله عليه وسلم, but the disbelievers and hypocrites who died in kufr will remain in Hellfire eternally.

A position rejected by al-Tahawi — though he addresses it implicitly — is that of those who held that Hellfire will eventually cease. This was a position attributed to some scholars, including Ibn Taymiyyah and Ibn al-Qayyim in certain of their writings, though this attribution is disputed. The mainstream Sunni position, as al-Tahawi articulates, is that both abodes are eternal and neither will perish.

The descriptions of Paradise in the Quran and Sunnah are affirmed literally and without forced metaphorical interpretation: rivers of water, milk, wine (which does not intoxicate), and honey; lush gardens and towering palaces; the meeting of loved ones; the hearing of الله's direct address; and above all else, the Beatific Vision of الله Himself.
